## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024]**  
Define **feature extraction** in the context of computational health informatics. Differentiate it from **feature selection** with a suitable biomedical example.

**Model Answer:**

*Feature extraction* is the process of deriving new, compact, and clinically meaningful numerical descriptors from raw biomedical data (signals, images, or records). For example, computing the **heart rate** from an ECG signal is a feature-extraction operation — a single new number summarizing thousands of raw voltage samples.

*Feature selection*, on the other hand, does not create new features; it merely picks a *subset* of *already-extracted* features that contribute most to a model. For example, from 50 features (HR, QRS duration, ST elevation, …), keeping only the 12 highest-ranked by mutual information is a *feature selection* step.

| Aspect | Feature Extraction | Feature Selection |
|---|---|---|
| Output | New features computed from raw data | Subset of existing features |
| Example in cardiology | Computing RMSSD from RR intervals | Choosing top-10 from 50 HRV indices |
| Reduces | Signal/image dimensionality | Feature-space cardinality |
| Number of features after | Can be >, =, or < original | Always < original |

(3 marks: definition 1, distinction table 2)

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**  
List any **six** time-domain features commonly extracted from an ECG signal. State the clinical interpretation of **RMS** in an EMG signal.

**Model Answer:**

Six time-domain features of an ECG signal are:

1. **Mean** — average voltage level (indicates baseline offset).
2. **Standard Deviation** — amplitude variability.
3. **Root Mean Square (RMS)** — overall signal power.
4. **Mean Absolute Value (MAV)** — robust mean amplitude.
5. **Skewness** — asymmetry of the amplitude distribution (e.g., QRS morphology abnormality).
6. **Zero Crossing Rate (ZCR)** — count of sign changes (crude frequency indicator).
7. **Hjorth Activity / Mobility / Complexity** — power / mean frequency / frequency change.

**Clinical interpretation of RMS in EMG:** RMS of a surface EMG (sEMG) signal is directly proportional to the **level of muscle activation / contraction force**. It is used in prosthetics control, rehabilitation monitoring, and ergonomics to quantify muscular effort — higher RMS ⇒ stronger contraction (assuming isotonic conditions).

(3 marks: list 1.5, interpretation 1.5)

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

> **KTU 2024 Rule:** Each Part B question carries 14 marks and has an internal choice. Both alternatives (Q-A and Q-B) are provided below; in the exam, a student answers **either** Q-A **or** Q-B.

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks) — Comprehensive on Time-Domain Feature Extraction**

> **[KTU University Exam — Model Paper, KTU 2024 Scheme]**  
> *(a)* Define the following features with formulas: **mean, variance, RMS, MAV, skewness, and kurtosis**. Explain the **clinical significance** of skewness and kurtosis in ECG analysis. (7 marks)  
> *(b)* For an EMG segment of length $N=5$ with samples $[0.1, 0.4, 0.7, 0.3, 0.2]$ (in mV), compute the **mean, variance, standard deviation, RMS, and MAV**. (7 marks)

**(a) Model Solution — Definitions and Clinical Significance (7 marks):**

| Feature | Formula | Clinical Significance in ECG |
|---|---|---|
| Mean | $\mu = \frac{1}{N}\sum_{i=1}^N x_i$ | DC baseline offset; should be ≈ 0 after filtering. Non-zero ⇒ baseline wander. |
| Variance | $\sigma^2 = \frac{1}{N-1}\sum(x_i-\mu)^2$ | Beat-to-beat amplitude variability. Used in HRV analysis. |
| RMS | $x_{rms} = \sqrt{\frac{1}{N}\sum x_i^2}$ | Overall signal energy. Important for QRS amplitude quantification. |
| MAV | $MAV = \frac{1}{N}\sum\vert x_i\vert$ | Robust mean amplitude, less sensitive to outliers than RMS. |
| Skewness | $S = \frac{1}{N}\sum\left(\frac{x_i-\mu}{\sigma}\right)^3$ | Measures asymmetry of amplitude distribution. **In ECG**, a *negative* skewness of the QRS region indicates an **abnormal ventricular depolarization pattern** (e.g., left bundle branch block, premature ventricular contractions). |
| Kurtosis | $K = \frac{1}{N}\sum\left(\frac{x_i-\mu}{\sigma}\right)^4$ | Measures "tailedness". A *high kurtosis* in ECG signals indicates **sharp, peaked QRS complexes** characteristic of hyperkalemia or ventricular tachycardia. A *low kurtosis* indicates a flattened waveform, as in myocardial ischemia. |

(7 marks: 1 per feature × 6 = 6, clinical interpretation of skewness + kurtosis 1 = 7)

**(b) Model Solution — Numerical Computation (7 marks):**

Given $\mathbf{x} = [0.1, 0.4, 0.7, 0.3, 0.2]$, $N=5$.

**Mean $\mu$:**

$$
\mu = \frac{0.1+0.4+0.7+0.3+0.2}{5} = \frac{1.7}{5} = 0.34 \text{ mV}
$$

**Centered signal $x_i - \mu$:**

$$
[-0.24,\; +0.06,\; +0.36,\; -0.04,\; -0.14]
$$

**Squared deviations $(x_i-\mu)^2$:**

$$
[0.0576,\; 0.0036,\; 0.1296,\; 0.0016,\; 0.0196] \quad \text{Sum} = 0.2120
$$

**Variance $\sigma^2$ (Bessel-corrected):**

$$
\sigma^2 = \frac{0.2120}{N-1} = \frac{0.2120}{4} = 0.0530 \text{ mV}^2
$$

**Standard Deviation $\sigma$:**

$$
\sigma = \sqrt{0.0530} \approx 0.2302 \text{ mV}
$$

**RMS:**

$$
x_{rms} = \sqrt{\frac{0.1^2 + 0.4^2 + 0.7^2 + 0.3^2 + 0.2^2}{5}} = \sqrt{\frac{0.01+0.16+0.49+0.09+0.04}{5}} = \sqrt{\frac{0.79}{5}} = \sqrt{0.158} \approx 0.3975 \text{ mV}
$$

**MAV:**

$$
MAV = \frac{\vert 0.1\vert + \vert 0.4\vert + \vert 0.7\vert + \vert 0.3\vert + \vert 0.2\vert}{5} = \frac{1.7}{5} = 0.34 \text{ mV}
$$

**Final answer block:**

| Feature | Value |
|---|---|
| Mean $\mu$ | **0.34 mV** |
| Variance $\sigma^2$ | **0.0530 mV²** |
| Std Dev $\sigma$ | **0.2302 mV** |
| RMS $x_{rms}$ | **0.3975 mV** |
| MAV | **0.34 mV** |

(7 marks: 1 for setup, 1 for mean, 1 for centered, 1 for variance, 1 for SD, 1 for RMS, 1 for MAV)

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — Dimensionality Reduction & Feature Representation**

> **[KTU University Exam — Model Paper, KTU 2024 Scheme]**  
> *(a)* Explain **Principal Component Analysis (PCA)** with its mathematical formulation. State and justify the variance-explained-ratio criterion for choosing the number of components in a healthcare dataset. (7 marks)  
> *(b)* Compare **PCA and LDA** as dimensionality-reduction techniques for clinical classification. Mention one biomedical application where LDA is preferred over PCA. (7 marks)

**(a) Model Solution — PCA Formulation (7 marks):**

**Goal of PCA:** Find orthogonal directions (principal components) along which the projected data has maximum variance. This compresses high-dimensional biomedical data (e.g., 20,000 gene-expression values) into a few informative axes.

**Mathematical formulation:**

1. **Center the data:** $\mathbf{X}_c = \mathbf{X} - \mathbf{1}\mu^T$, where $\mu$ is the mean feature vector.

2. **Compute the empirical covariance matrix:**
$$\mathbf{C} = \frac{1}{N-1} \mathbf{X}_c^T \mathbf{X}_c \in \mathbb{R}^{d \times d}$$

3. **Solve the eigenvalue problem:**
$$\mathbf{C} \mathbf{v}_k = \lambda_k \mathbf{v}_k, \quad k=1,2,\dots,d$$
where $\lambda_1 \geq \lambda_2 \geq \dots \geq \lambda_d \geq 0$.

4. **Form the projection matrix** $\mathbf{V}_k = [\mathbf{v}_1, \dots, \mathbf{v}_k]$ and project: $\mathbf{Z} = \mathbf{X}_c \mathbf{V}_k$.

**Variance-explained-ratio criterion:**

$$
VE_k = \frac{\sum_{i=1}^{k} \lambda_i}{\sum_{i=1}^{d} \lambda_i}
$$

**Justification for healthcare:**  
- PCA is **unsupervised**; it ignores class labels. In healthcare, datasets are often expensive and small; retaining 95% of variance (i.e., choosing smallest $k$ such that $VE_k \geq 0.95$) is a standard, label-free way to choose $k$.  
- It also guards against overfitting in downstream classifiers by removing noise directions (small $\lambda_i$ correspond to noise).  
- For interpretability, a **scree plot** of $\lambda_i$ vs. $i$ visually shows the "elbow" — the $k$ after which eigenvalues plateau corresponds to noise.

(7 marks: 2 for goal, 3 for math, 2 for VE justification)

**(b) Model Solution — PCA vs. LDA Comparison (7 marks):**

| Aspect | PCA | LDA |
|---|---|---|
| Supervised? | **No** (unsupervised) | **Yes** (uses class labels) |
| Objective | Maximize **variance** projection | Maximize **between-class / within-class scatter ratio** |
| Max output dim | $d$ (original) | $C-1$ (number of classes − 1) |
| Data assumption | Gaussian not required; linear | Multivariate Gaussian with equal covariance |
| Best for | Compression, denoising, visualization | **Classification** |
| Class-label usage | None | Required |
| Computational cost | Eigendecomposition of $d\times d$ | Eigendecomposition of $\mathbf{S}_W^{-1}\mathbf{S}_B$ |
| Limitation | May discard discriminative low-variance directions | Fails if $\mathbf{S}_W$ is singular (e.g., $N \ll d$) |

**Biomedical application where LDA is preferred:**  
**Tumor classification from gene-expression data (e.g., Leukaemia dataset — AML vs. ALL).**  
The dataset has $N \approx 72$ patients but $d \approx 7,000$ genes. PCA is used for initial exploration, but LDA projects onto a single axis ($C-1 = 1$) that **maximally separates the two cancer subtypes**. The resulting 1-D score is used as a single, clinically meaningful *diagnostic index* and can be thresholded to make a binary diagnosis. LDA is preferred because it uses the *labels* (cancer subtype) that PCA discards.

(7 marks: 5 for comparison table, 2 for application)

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common places students lose marks in this topic (KTU 2024):**
> 1. **Confusing "extraction" with "selection"** in definitions (costs the 1-mark difference in 3-mark Q1).
> 2. **Forgetting Bessel's correction** ($N-1$ vs. $N$ in variance). KTU examiners *do* check this; using $N$ in place of $N-1$ in a numerical may cost 0.5–1 mark.
> 3. **Skipping units** (e.g., mV, mV², bpm). Always carry units; they earn easy partial credit.
> 4. **Not normalizing the data before PCA** — PCA is *not* rotation-invariant to feature scaling. If you skip standardization, the eigenvector will be dominated by the largest-magnitude feature.
> 5. **Mixing up PCA and LDA objectives** — PCA maximizes variance, LDA maximizes the Fisher ratio $\frac{\mathbf{W}^T\mathbf{S}_B\mathbf{W}}{\mathbf{W}^T\mathbf{S}_W\mathbf{W}}$. A common slip is to claim "PCA maximizes class separation" — it does not.
> 6. **In a 14-mark Q, skipping the clinical interpretation** of features. KTU 2024 explicitly rewards *biomedical relevance* of ML techniques. A purely mathematical answer without the *why* loses 1–2 marks.
> 7. **Forgetting to re-shape** the feature vector consistently: train-test split must use the *same* scaling parameters (μ, σ) computed on the training set. Re-fitting on test set is a leakage error.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist for "Feature Extraction and Representation":**

- **Definition (must be first sentence of any answer):** Feature extraction transforms raw biomedical data into a compact, discriminative numerical feature vector $\mathbf{x} \in \mathbb{R}^d$.
- **Pipeline order:** Acquisition → Pre-processing → Segmentation → **Feature Extraction** → Normalization → Dimensionality Reduction → Model.
- **Three categorical pillars:**
  1. **Time-domain** — mean, variance, RMS, MAV, ZCR, Hjorth, skewness, kurtosis.
  2. **Frequency-domain** — PSD, peak/median frequency, band power, spectral entropy.
  3. **Time-frequency / non-linear** — STFT, wavelets, sample entropy, fractal dimension, Poincaré indices.
- **Always normalize** before PCA/SVM/k-NN: Z-score for parametric, Min-Max for neural networks, Robust for outlier-heavy clinical data.
- **PCA is unsupervised, linear, variance-maximizing; LDA is supervised, class-separating, capped at $C-1$ dimensions.**
- **Variance-explained rule of thumb:** $VE_k \geq 0.95$ for healthcare datasets.
- **Image features** rely on shape (area, perimeter), texture (GLCM, LBP), and modern deep embeddings (CNN features from ResNet/VGG).
- **Sequence features** (ECG, EEG) can be both hand-crafted (statistical) and learned (1-D CNN, LSTM, Transformer).
- **For 14-mark Qs in KTU 2024:** always include (i) formulas, (ii) numerical worked example, (iii) clinical interpretation, (iv) one biomedical use-case.
- **Memorize the Formula Sheet** in Section 2.5 — the seven formulas on skewness, kurtosis, RMS, MAV, ZCR, Z-score, and PCA VE ratio are tested every cycle.
- **Units to remember:** mV (voltage), ms (time), bpm (rate), Hz (frequency), mV²/Hz (PSD), bits (entropy), % (oxygen saturation).

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Supervised and Unsupervised Learning Algorithms
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEADT751 - COMPUTATIONAL HEALTH INFORMATICS
> - **Module:** Module Module 3
> - **Topic:** Supervised and Unsupervised Learning Algorithms

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What is Machine Learning in Health Informatics?

**Machine Learning (ML)** is a subfield of Artificial Intelligence (AI) that enables computational systems to learn patterns from historical healthcare data and make intelligent predictions or decisions *without being explicitly programmed* for every scenario. In the context of **Computational Health Informatics**, ML algorithms are trained on Electronic Health Records (EHR), medical imaging datasets, genomic sequences, and clinical trial data to assist in **diagnosis, prognosis, drug discovery, and personalized treatment planning**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:** Machine Learning is the scientific study of statistical models and algorithms that a computer system uses to progressively improve its performance on a specific task by leveraging training data, *without* its operational logic being statically defined.

---

## 1.2 The Two Principal Paradigms of Learning

ML algorithms are broadly partitioned into three families based on the **nature of the supervisory signal** available during training:

### A. Supervised Learning
The algorithm is trained on a **labeled dataset**, meaning every input sample is paired with a known correct output (the "ground truth" label, often provided by a clinician or radiologist). The model learns a mapping function $f: X \rightarrow Y$ that minimizes prediction error against the labels.

**Conceptual Analogy — "The Medical Resident":**
> Imagine a fresh MBBS intern learning to read chest X-rays. The senior consultant (the *supervisor*) shows the intern 10,000 X-rays, each *already labeled* as "Normal" or "Pneumonia" or "Tuberculosis." By studying the labeled examples repeatedly, the intern's brain builds an internal pattern-matching function. When shown a *new, unlabeled* X-ray, the intern can now confidently predict the diagnosis. The intern is performing **supervised learning** — the answers (labels) were provided during training, and the goal is to generalize that learning to unseen cases.

**Health Informatics Examples:**
- Classifying a skin lesion image as *Benign* vs *Malignant*.
- Predicting a patient's 30-day readmission risk (**Yes/No**).
- Estimating blood glucose level from continuous CGM sensor streams (**continuous value**).

### B. Unsupervised Learning
The algorithm is given an **unlabeled dataset** and must discover the underlying structure, groupings, or low-dimensional representation on its own. There is no "ground truth" — only the intrinsic statistical properties of the data guide the discovery process.

**Conceptual Analogy — "The Microbiologist with a Petri Dish":**
> A microbiologist receives 200 bacterial colony samples with no idea how they should be classified. The scientist pours the samples into a dish, observes which colonies *cluster naturally together* based on shape, color, and growth rate, and discovers (without prior labels) that there are *three distinct species* hidden in the mix. The microbiologist has performed **unsupervised clustering** — no labels were given; the structure emerged purely from the data.

**Health Informatics Examples:**
- Grouping patients into **novel disease subtypes** based on gene expression.
- Detecting **anomalous patient vitals** that do not fit any known clinical pattern.
- Compressing high-dimensional MRI scans into 2D for visual inspection.

> [!NOTE]
> **Reinforcement Learning (RL)** is the third paradigm, where an agent learns by interacting with an environment and receiving rewards/penalties (e.g., AI-controlled insulin pump titration). It is *not* the focus of Module 3 but may appear as a Part A conceptual question.

---

## 1.3 Tabular Comparison: Supervised vs. Unsupervised Learning

| Attribute | Supervised Learning | Unsupervised Learning |
|---|---|---|
| **Training Data** | Labeled $(x_i, y_i)$ pairs | Unlabeled $x_i$ only |
| **Learning Objective** | Learn $f: X \rightarrow Y$ | Discover $P(X)$, the data distribution |
| **Feedback Signal** | Explicit error vs. true label | None (intrinsic structure only) |
| **Core Tasks** | Classification, Regression | Clustering, Dimensionality Reduction, Association |
| **Evaluation Metric** | Accuracy, F1, AUC-ROC, RMSE | Silhouette Score, Davies-Bouldin Index, Reconstruction Error |
| **Clinical Analogy** | Resident learning from labeled scans | Microbiologist discovering unknown colony types |
| **Data Cost** | **High** (labeling by experts is expensive) | **Low** (no labels required) |
| **Output Type** | Predicted label or value | Group IDs, compressed representations, rules |

---

## 1.4 Why This Topic is Critical for KTU Examinations

> [!IMPORTANT]
> As per the **KTU 2024 Scheme Bloom's Taxonomy mapping**, this topic primarily tests **CO3: Apply computational intelligence techniques to model healthcare data**, across cognitive levels **L2 (Understand)** and **L3 (Apply)**. A 14-mark Part B question will *almost always* ask you to: (i) classify a problem as supervised or unsupervised, (ii) choose a suitable algorithm, and (iii) either walk through its steps on a small health dataset OR contrast two algorithms on a clinical use case.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Supervised Learning — Algorithmic Taxonomy

Supervised learning is further split based on the **type of the target variable $Y$**:

### 2.1.1 Classification (Target $Y$ is categorical / discrete)
The model predicts which of $C$ discrete classes an input belongs to.

**(a) Logistic Regression**
Despite the name, it is a **classification** algorithm. It models the probability of class membership using the **sigmoid (logistic) function**:

$$P(Y=1 \mid X) = \sigma(z) = \frac{1}{1 + e^{-z}}, \quad \text{where} \quad z = \beta_0 + \sum_{j=1}^{n} \beta_j x_j$$

**Clinical Use:** Predicting 10-year cardiovascular disease risk from age, BP, cholesterol, and smoking status (Framingham Risk Score analog).

**(b) k-Nearest Neighbors (k-NN)**
A non-parametric, instance-based learner. To classify a new patient, find the **$k$ most similar historical patients** (by distance metric, typically Euclidean) and assign the majority class label among them.

$$\hat{y} = \text{mode}\{y_i : x_i \in \mathcal{N}_k(x_{\text{new}})\}$$

**Clinical Use:** Early diabetes detection using biometric similarity to known diabetic cohorts.

**(c) Decision Trees (CART / ID3 / C4.5)**
A tree-structured classifier that recursively partitions the feature space using **information-theoretic criteria**. At each node, the algorithm selects the feature $X_j$ and split threshold $t$ that **maximizes Information Gain** (or equivalently, minimizes Gini impurity / entropy).

$$G_{\text{Info}}(X_j, t) = H(\mathcal{S}) - \sum_{v \in \{L, R\}} \frac{\mid \mathcal{S}_v \mid}{\mid \mathcal{S} \mid} H(\mathcal{S}_v)$$

where the **Shannon Entropy** is:

$$H(\mathcal{S}) = -\sum_{c=1}^{C} p_c \log_2 p_c$$

**Clinical Use:** Generating interpretable clinical decision support rules (e.g., "If BP > 140 AND Glucose > 180 → High Stroke Risk").

**(d) Naive Bayes Classifier**
A probabilistic classifier based on **Bayes' Theorem** with a "naive" assumption of conditional feature independence:

$$P(Y=c \mid X) = \frac{P(Y=c) \cdot \prod_{j=1}^{n} P(X_j \mid Y=c)}{P(X)}$$

The predicted class is $\hat{y} = \arg\max_c P(Y=c \mid X)$.

**Clinical Use:** Spam filtering in clinical emails; real-time text classification of radiology reports.

**(e) Support Vector Machine (SVM)**
Finds the **maximum-margin hyperplane** separating classes in (possibly high-dimensional) feature space. For non-linearly separable data, the **kernel trick** projects data into a higher-dimensional space:

$$\max_{\mathbf{w}, b} \quad \frac{2}{\lVert \mathbf{w} \rVert} \quad \text{s.t.} \quad y_i (\mathbf{w}^\top \mathbf{x}_i + b) \geq 1, \quad \forall i$$

**Clinical Use:** Cancer tissue classification from microarray gene expression (high-dimensional, small sample size — SVM's forte).

**(f) Random Forest (Ensemble of Decision Trees)**
Constructs $B$ decision trees on **bootstrap resamples** of the training set, with **random feature subsets** at each split. Final prediction is the **majority vote** (classification) or **average** (regression).

$$\hat{y}_{\text{RF}} = \text{mode}\{T_b(\mathbf{x}) : b = 1, 2, \ldots, B\}$$

**Clinical Use:** Predicting ICU mortality from multi-variate time-series vitals; highly robust to missing EHR data.

---

### 2.1.2 Regression (Target $Y$ is continuous)
The model predicts a numeric value.

**(a) Linear Regression**
Models the relationship as a linear combination of features:

$$\hat{y} = \beta_0 + \sum_{j=1}^{n} \beta_j x_j + \epsilon$$

Coefficients are estimated by **Ordinary Least Squares (OLS)** — minimizing the sum of squared residuals:

$$\min_{\beta} \sum_{i=1}^{m} (y_i - \hat{y}_i)^2$$

**Closed-form solution (Normal Equation):**

$$\boldsymbol{\beta} = (X^\top X)^{-1} X^\top \mathbf{y}$$

**Clinical Use:** Modeling the dose-response curve of a drug; predicting patient Length-of-Stay (LOS).

**(b) Polynomial Regression** — extension using $x^2, x^3, \ldots$ terms for non-linear relationships.

---

## 2.2 Unsupervised Learning — Algorithmic Taxonomy

### 2.2.1 Clustering Algorithms

**(a) K-Means Clustering**
Partitions $m$ data points into $K$ disjoint clusters by iteratively:
1. **Initialization:** Place $K$ centroids $\{\mu_1, \ldots, \mu_K\}$ (e.g., K-Means++).
2. **Assignment Step:** Assign each point to the nearest centroid.
3. **Update Step:** Recompute centroids as the mean of assigned points.
4. **Repeat** until convergence.

**Objective (Within-Cluster Sum of Squares — WCSS):**

$$J = \sum_{k=1}^{K} \sum_{\mathbf{x}_i \in C_k} \lVert \mathbf{x}_i - \boldsymbol{\mu}_k \rVert^2$$

**Clinical Use:** Stratifying Type-2 diabetes patients into 3 distinct phenotypic subgroups for personalized therapy.

**(b) Hierarchical Clustering**
Builds a **dendrogram** (tree of clusters) using either:
- **Agglomerative (bottom-up):** Each point starts as its own cluster; iteratively merge the two closest clusters.
- **Divisive (top-down):** All points start in one cluster; iteratively split.

**Linkage criteria** (distance between clusters $C_a$ and $C_b$):
- Single: $\min \lVert x_i - x_j \rVert$ for $x_i \in C_a, x_j \in C_b$
- Complete: $\max \lVert x_i - x_j \rVert$
- Average: $\frac{1}{\mid C_a \mid \cdot \mid C_b \mid} \sum \sum \lVert x_i - x_j \rVert$

**Clinical Use:** Taxonomy construction of diseases from symptom co-occurrence.

**(c) DBSCAN (Density-Based Spatial Clustering of Applications with Noise)**
Groups together points that are **densely packed**, marking points in low-density regions as **outliers** (noise). Requires two parameters: $\epsilon$ (neighborhood radius) and $\text{MinPts}$ (minimum neighbors).

**Clinical Use:** Detecting anomalous ECG beats (rare arrhythmias) embedded in normal sinus rhythm.

### 2.2.2 Dimensionality Reduction

**(a) Principal Component Analysis (PCA)**
An orthogonal linear transformation that projects data onto the directions of **maximum variance** (principal components). The principal components are the **eigenvectors** of the data covariance matrix.

Given centered data matrix $X \in \mathbb{R}^{m \times n}$:

$$\Sigma = \frac{1}{m-1} X^\top X \quad \text{(covariance matrix)}$$

The $k$-th principal component $\mathbf{v}_k$ is the eigenvector of $\Sigma$ corresponding to the $k$-th largest eigenvalue $\lambda_k$.

**Variance explained by PC $k$:**

$$\text{Explained Variance Ratio}_k = \frac{\lambda_k}{\sum_{j=1}^{n} \lambda_j}$$

**Clinical Use:** Visualizing high-dimensional gene expression data on a 2D scatter plot to reveal tumor subtype clusters.

**(b) t-SNE (t-Distributed Stochastic Neighbor Embedding)**
A **non-linear** technique superior to PCA for visualizing high-dimensional data in 2D/3D. It models pairwise similarities as probability distributions and minimizes the **Kullback-Leibler (KL) divergence** between the high-D and low-D distributions.

**Clinical Use:** Visualizing single-cell RNA-sequencing (scRNA-seq) clusters — the de-facto standard in modern bioinformatics.

---

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> The following table is the **rapid-revision cheat sheet** for the exam hall. Memorize the equations, the boundary conditions, and the clinical mapping.

| # | Algorithm | Family | Core Formula / Objective | Key Hyperparameters | Clinical Application |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Linear Regression** | Supervised (Regression) | $\hat{y} = \beta_0 + \sum \beta_j x_j$ | — | Drug dose-response modeling |
| 2 | **Logistic Regression** | Supervised (Classification) | $P(Y=1 \mid X) = \frac{1}{1 + e^{-z}}$ | Regularization $\lambda$ | Cardiac risk stratification |
| 3 | **k-NN** | Supervised (Both) | $\hat{y} = \text{mode}\{y_i : x_i \in \mathcal{N}_k\}$ | $k$, distance metric | Early diabetes detection |
| 4 | **Decision Tree** | Supervised (Both) | Maximize $G_{\text{Info}}$ / Minimize $H(\mathcal{S})$ | Max depth, min samples/leaf | Interpretable clinical rules |
| 5 | **Naive Bayes** | Supervised (Classification) | $\hat{y} = \arg\max_c P(Y=c) \prod P(X_j \mid Y=c)$ | Prior smoothing $\alpha$ | Radiology report classification |
| 6 | **SVM** | Supervised (Classification) | $\max \frac{2}{\lVert \mathbf{w} \rVert}$ s.t. $y_i(\mathbf{w}^\top x_i + b) \geq 1$ | Kernel, $C$, $\gamma$ | Cancer genomics |
| 7 | **Random Forest** | Supervised (Ensemble) | $\hat{y} = \text{mode}\{T_b(\mathbf{x})\}_{b=1}^{B}$ | $B$, max features, depth | ICU mortality prediction |
| 8 | **K-Means** | Unsupervised (Clustering) | $\min J = \sum_k \sum_{x_i \in C_k} \lVert x_i - \mu_k \rVert^2$ | $K$ | Diabetes phenotype stratification |
| 9 | **Hierarchical** | Unsupervised (Clustering) | Dendrogram + linkage criterion | Linkage, distance threshold | Disease taxonomy from symptoms |
| 10 | **DBSCAN** | Unsupervised (Clustering) | Density-reachability: $\geq \text{MinPts}$ within $\epsilon$ | $\epsilon$, $\text{MinPts}$ | Anomalous ECG beat detection |
| 11 | **PCA** | Unsupervised (Dim. Reduction) | Eigen-decomposition of $\Sigma$ | # components $k$ | Gene expression visualization |
| 12 | **t-SNE** | Unsupervised (Dim. Reduction) | Minimize $\text{KL}(P \mid\mid Q)$ | Perplexity, learning rate | scRNA-seq cluster visualization |

> [!WARNING]
> **Units / Scale Sensitivity:** PCA, K-Means, and SVM with RBF kernel are **distance-based** and therefore highly sensitive to feature scale. In KTU numerical problems, **always apply StandardScaler** ($z = (x - \mu)/\sigma$) before these algorithms or you will be marked down.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

In the modern health-tech industry, this taxonomy maps directly onto deployed products:
- **Wearable ECG monitors (e.g., Apple Watch, AliveCor):** 1-D CNN + SVM for atrial fibrillation detection (supervised).
- **Hospital patient flow systems:** K-Means clustering to group patients by acuity for resource scheduling.
- **Pharma R&D pipelines:** Random Forest for virtual drug screening across millions of compounds.
- **Medical imaging AI (PathAI, Aidoc):** Deep ensembles of CNNs (a hierarchical extension of these classical algorithms) for tumor segmentation.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

This section works through the **complete step-by-step derivations** of the three most examination-frequent algorithms: Logistic Regression (Maximum Likelihood), K-Means (objective convergence), and PCA (eigendecomposition). Each step contains explicit text explaining the mathematical conversion logic.

---

## 3.1 Derivation: Logistic Regression via Maximum Likelihood Estimation (MLE)

**Context (Health Problem):** A hospital wants to build a model predicting the probability of a patient developing **Type-2 Diabetes ($Y=1$)** within 5 years, using predictors: BMI ($x_1$), Age ($x_2$), and Family History ($x_3 \in \{0,1\}$).

**Step 1 — Model the linear combination (the logit):**
We begin by forming a linear combination of the predictors, which is the *unbounded* raw score the model will pass through the sigmoid.

$$z_i = \beta_0 + \beta_1 x_{1i} + \beta_2 x_{2i} + \beta_3 x_{3i}$$

**Step 2 — Apply the sigmoid to obtain a probability bounded in $(0, 1)$:**
The raw score $z_i$ can range over $(-\infty, +\infty)$. To convert it to a valid probability, we apply the **logistic (sigmoid) function**.

$$P(Y=1 \mid \mathbf{x}_i) = \hat{p}_i = \frac{1}{1 + e^{-z_i}}$$

**Step 3 — Express the Bernoulli likelihood of a single observation:**
Since $Y$ is binary, each observation follows a **Bernoulli distribution**. The probability of observing the actual label $y_i$ (which is 0 or 1) under the model is:

$$P(y_i \mid \mathbf{x}_i, \boldsymbol{\beta}) = \hat{p}_i^{y_i} (1 - \hat{p}_i)^{1 - y_i}$$

**Why this form?** Because if $y_i = 1$, only $\hat{p}_i$ matters (the first term), and if $y_i = 0$, only $(1 - \hat{p}_i)$ matters (the second term). The exponents select the right term automatically.

**Step 4 — Write the joint likelihood over all $m$ independent patients:**
Assuming the $m$ patient records are i.i.d., the joint likelihood is the product of the individual likelihoods.

$$\mathcal{L}(\boldsymbol{\beta}) = \prod_{i=1}^{m} \hat{p}_i^{y_i} (1 - \hat{p}_i)^{1 - y_i}$$

**Step 5 — Take the natural logarithm (log-likelihood) to convert products into sums:**
Logarithms are monotonically increasing, so maximizing the log-likelihood is equivalent to maximizing the likelihood itself. The log transformation converts the multiplicative product into a much easier-to-differentiate sum.

$$\ell(\boldsymbol{\beta}) = \ln \mathcal{L}(\boldsymbol{\beta}) = \sum_{i=1}^{m} \Big[ y_i \ln \hat{p}_i + (1 - y_i) \ln (1 - \hat{p}_i) \Big]$$

**Step 6 — Substitute $\hat{p}_i = \sigma(z_i)$ and simplify using the sigmoid identity:**
Using $\ln \sigma(z) = -\ln(1 + e^{-z})$ and the algebraic identity of the sigmoid, the log-likelihood can be rewritten in the cleaner form:

$$\ell(\boldsymbol{\beta}) = \sum_{i=1}^{m} \Big[ y_i z_i - \ln(1 + e^{z_i}) \Big]$$

**Step 7 — Compute the gradient with respect to $\beta_j$ and set to zero:**
Differentiating $\ell$ with respect to $\beta_j$ yields the first-order condition. The derivative leverages the elegant property of the sigmoid: $\frac{\partial \sigma}{\partial z} = \sigma(z)(1 - \sigma(z))$.

$$\frac{\partial \ell}{\partial \beta_j} = \sum_{i=1}^{m} (y_i - \hat{p}_i) x_{ij} = 0$$

**Step 8 — Observe there is no closed-form solution; use gradient ascent:**
Unlike linear regression, the system of equations from Step 7 is **non-linear in $\boldsymbol{\beta}$** (because $\hat{p}_i$ itself depends on $\boldsymbol{\beta}$). We therefore iteratively update:

$$\boldsymbol{\beta}^{(t+1)} = \boldsymbol{\beta}^{(t)} + \eta \cdot \nabla_{\boldsymbol{\beta}} \ell$$

where $\eta$ is the **learning rate**. In practice, modern frameworks use **L-BFGS** or **Newton-Raphson** (Iteratively Reweighted Least Squares — IRLS) for faster convergence.

> [!NOTE]
> **Final MLE Estimate for Health Use:** After convergence, the hospital can now compute $P(\text{Diabetes} \mid \text{BMI, Age, FamilyHistory})$ for any new patient by substituting into Step 2.

---

## 3.2 Derivation: K-Means Objective Convergence Proof (Lloyd's Algorithm)

**Context (Health Problem):** A hospital has 6 patient records with two features (Systolic BP, Cholesterol). We want to group them into $K=2$ risk clusters.

| Patient | BP ($x_1$) | Chol ($x_2$) |
|---|---|---|
| P1 | 120 | 200 |
| P2 | 125 | 205 |
| P3 | 130 | 210 |
| P4 | 160 | 280 |
| P5 | 165 | 285 |
| P6 | 170 | 290 |

**Step 1 — Initialize centroids (K-Means++ for stability):**
Choose P1 and P4 as initial centroids:
- $\mu_1^{(0)} = (120, 200)$
- $\mu_2^{(0)} = (160, 280)$

**Step 2 — Compute Euclidean distance from every point to every centroid (Iteration 1, Assignment Step):**

$$d(\mathbf{x}_i, \mu_k) = \sqrt{(x_{1i} - \mu_{k1})^2 + (x_{2i} - \mu_{k2})^2}$$

Calculations:
- P1 to $\mu_1$: $\sqrt{0 + 0} = 0$ → Cluster 1
- P2 to $\mu_1$: $\sqrt{25 + 25} = \sqrt{50} \approx 7.07$ → Cluster 1
- P2 to $\mu_2$: $\sqrt{35^2 + 75^2} = \sqrt{6850} \approx 82.76$ (larger, so P2 → Cluster 1)
- P3 to $\mu_1$: $\sqrt{100 + 100} = \sqrt{200} \approx 14.14$ → Cluster 1
- P3 to $\mu_2$: $\sqrt{30^2 + 70^2} \approx 76.16$ (larger)
- P4 to $\mu_2$: 0 → Cluster 2
- P5 to $\mu_2$: $\sqrt{25 + 25} \approx 7.07$ → Cluster 2
- P6 to $\mu_2$: $\sqrt{100 + 100} \approx 14.14$ → Cluster 2

**Assignment after Iteration 1:**
- $C_1 = \{P1, P2, P3\}$
- $C_2 = \{P4, P5, P6\}$

**Step 3 — Update centroids (Update Step) by computing the mean of each cluster:**

$$\mu_k^{(1)} = \frac{1}{\mid C_k \mid} \sum_{\mathbf{x}_i \in C_k} \mathbf{x}_i$$

- $\mu_1^{(1)} = \left( \frac{120+125+130}{3}, \frac{200+205+210}{3} \right) = (125, 205)$
- $\mu_2^{(1)} = \left( \frac{160+165+170}{3}, \frac{280+285+290}{3} \right) = (165, 285)$

**Step 4 — Recompute distances (Iteration 2):**
The points are clearly equidistant-or-closer to their respective centroids. Assignments remain unchanged. Centroids remain at $(125, 205)$ and $(165, 285)$.

**Step 5 — Verify convergence using the WCSS objective:**

$$J^{(1)} = \sum_{i \in C_1} \lVert \mathbf{x}_i - \mu_1 \rVert^2 + \sum_{i \in C_2} \lVert \mathbf{x}_i - \mu_2 \rVert^2$$

For P1: $(120-125)^2 + (200-205)^2 = 25 + 25 = 50$
For P2: $(125-125)^2 + (205-205)^2 = 0$
For P3: $(130-125)^2 + (210-205)^2 = 25 + 25 = 50$
For P4: 0
For P5: 0
For P6: 50

$$J^{(1)} = 50 + 0 + 50 + 0 + 0 + 50 = 150$$

**Step 6 — Prove that $J$ monotonically decreases (the convergence guarantee):**
The two alternating steps are individually optimal:
- **Assignment step** minimizes $J$ with respect to cluster assignments (each point goes to its nearest centroid).
- **Update step** minimizes $J$ with respect to centroid positions (the mean is the L2-norm minimizer).

Since $J$ is bounded below by 0, this monotonic decrease guarantees convergence to a (local) minimum.

**Final Cluster Interpretation:**
- **Cluster 1** = "Low Risk" patients (BP ~125, Chol ~205).
- **Cluster 2** = "High Risk" patients (BP ~165, Chol ~285).

---

## 3.3 Derivation: Principal Component Analysis (PCA) via Eigendecomposition

**Step 1 — Center the data** (subtract the column mean $\bar{\mathbf{x}}$ from each feature). This is essential so that PC1 aligns with maximum variance rather than the mean offset.

$$\tilde{X} = X - \mathbf{1} \bar{\mathbf{x}}^\top$$

**Step 2 — Compute the $n \times n$ covariance matrix:**
For standardized data, the covariance captures how features co-vary. The diagonal entries are the variances of each feature, and the off-diagonal entries are the pairwise covariances.

$$\Sigma = \frac{1}{m-1} \tilde{X}^\top \tilde{X}$$

**Step 3 — Solve the eigenvalue equation:**
The principal components are the directions $\mathbf{v}$ in which the variance of the projected data is maximized. The Rayleigh quotient tells us that the variance of $\tilde{X} \mathbf{v}$ is $\mathbf{v}^\top \Sigma \mathbf{v}$. Maximizing this subject to $\lVert \mathbf{v} \rVert = 1$ yields the eigenvalue equation.

$$\Sigma \mathbf{v} = \lambda \mathbf{v}$$

**Step 4 — Solve $\det(\Sigma - \lambda I) = 0$ for eigenvalues $\lambda_1 \geq \lambda_2 \geq \ldots \geq \lambda_n \geq 0$.**

**Step 5 — For each $\lambda_k$, solve $(\Sigma - \lambda_k I) \mathbf{v}_k = \mathbf{0}$ to get the eigenvector $\mathbf{v}_k$.**

**Step 6 — Form the projection matrix $W = [\mathbf{v}_1, \mathbf{v}_2, \ldots, \mathbf{v}_k]$ using the top-$k$ eigenvectors. The low-dimensional representation is:**

$$Z = \tilde{X} W \in \mathbb{R}^{m \times k}$$

**Step 7 — Verify the variance captured equals the sum of top-$k$ eigenvalues:**

$$\text{Total Variance Captured} = \frac{\sum_{j=1}^{k} \lambda_j}{\sum_{j=1}^{n} \lambda_j}$$

A common KTU numerical problem gives you a 2×2 covariance matrix and asks you to compute the principal component. The workflow above is your complete answer template.

---

| Criterion | Logistic Regression | k-NN | Decision Tree | SVM | Random Forest | K-Means | PCA |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Learning Type** | Supervised | Supervised | Supervised | Supervised | Supervised | Unsupervised | Unsupervised |
| **Training Speed** | Fast | None (lazy) | Fast | Slow on large data | Slow (many trees) | Fast | Fast (SVD) |
| **Prediction Speed** | Fast | Slow ($O(mn)$) | Fast | Fast | Slow (vote) | N/A | N/A |
| **Handles Non-Linearity** | ✗ | ✓ | ✓ | ✓ (kernel) | ✓ | ✓ (spherical) | ✗ (linear) |
| **Interpretability** | High (coefficients) | Low | **Very High** (rules) | Low | Medium (feature importance) | Medium | Low (loadings) |
| **Robust to Outliers** | Moderate | Low | Moderate | Moderate | **High** | Low (mean shift) | Moderate |
| **High-Dimensional Data** | OK | Poor | OK | **Excellent** | Good | Poor | **Excellent** |
| **Requires Scaling** | Yes | **Yes** | No | **Yes** | No | **Yes** | **Yes** |
| **Health Use Fit** | Risk scores | Diagnostic lookup | Clinical guidelines | Genomics | EHR mortality | Phenotyping | scRNA-seq |

Always tie your choice to **three pillars**: data type (labeled vs unlabeled), data size, and clinical interpretability requirement.

## 4.3 K-Means Clustering Iterative Process (Block Architecture)

This diagram illustrates the Lloyd's algorithm cycle for a patient stratification use case.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S1[Input: m Patient Records<br>with n Features] --> S2[Step 1: Initialize K Centroids<br>using K-Means++]
    S2 --> S3[Step 2: Assignment Step<br>Assign each patient to nearest centroid]
    S3 --> S4[Step 3: Update Step<br>Recompute centroid = mean of cluster]
    S4 --> S5[Step 4: Compute WCSS Objective J]
    S5 --> S6{Convergence?<br>J unchanged OR<br>assignments stable}

```

## 4.4 PCA Block Architecture (Dimensionality Reduction Pipeline)

This diagram shows the mathematical pipeline for reducing a 10,000-dimensional gene expression vector into 2D for visualization.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P1[Gene Expression Matrix<br>500 patients x 10000 genes] --> P2[Step 1: Center Data<br>Subtract column means]
    P2 --> P3[Step 2: Compute Covariance Matrix<br>Sigma equals X transpose X by m minus 1]
    P3 --> P4[Step 3: Eigendecomposition<br>Solve Sigma v equals lambda v]
    P4 --> P5[Step 4: Sort Eigenvalues<br>Descending Order]
    P5 --> P6[Step 5: Select Top k Eigenvectors<br>where k much less than n]

```

## 4.5 Decision Tree Construction Flow (Clinical Rule Mining)

This diagram depicts the recursive partitioning process used to derive *interpretable* clinical decision rules from training data.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    T1[Root Node: All m Patients] --> T2[Test Feature Xj at Split Threshold t]
    T2 --> T3[Compute Gini Impurity or Entropy for Split]
    T3 --> T4[Compute Information Gain IG]
    T4 --> T5{Select Best Feature Xj Star and Threshold t Star}
    T5 --> T6[Split Node into Left Child and Right Child]
    T6 --> T7A[Left Subset: Patients where Xj less than t]
    T6 --> T7B[Right Subset: Patients where Xj greater or equal t]
    T7A --> T8{Stopping Criterion Met?<br>Max depth OR pure node OR min samples}
    T7B --> T8
    T8 -- No --> T2
    T8 -- Yes --> T9[Leaf Node: Assign Class Label by Majority Vote]
```
